title: Spin relaxation in a single-electron graphene quantum dot
classification_notes: GPT error: Unterminated string starting at: line 212 column 5 (char 12823)

paper_analysis.summary: This paper studies spin relaxation in a single-electron bilayer graphene quantum dot (BLG QD) using pulsed-gate transport spectroscopy at millikelvin temperatures and finite out-of-plane magnetic field. The authors extract spin relaxation times T1 exceeding 200 μs at 1.9 T, with a strong field dependence consistent with much longer lifetimes at lower fields, making BLG a promising platform for spin qubits.

figures.description: False-color SEM image of the gate-defined BLG quantum dot device layout, showing split gates, finger gates, source/drain, and bias-tee connections.
Schematic band diagram along the channel illustrating how a finger gate forms the quantum dot and how a neighboring gate tunes the tunnel barrier asymmetry.
Calculated/illustrated single-particle energy spectrum of the first orbital state versus out-of-plane magnetic field, including spin-orbit splitting and valley/spin Zeeman shifts.
Finite-bias spectroscopy map of the N=0 to 1 electron transition at B⊥ = 0.5 T, resolving the single-particle states.
Extracted energy splitting ΔE between the two K0 valley-polarized spin states as a function of B⊥, used to determine ΔSO and gs.
Square-pulse timing scheme for transient current spectroscopy, showing initialization and measurement windows and the possible tunneling/relaxation processes.
Current map versus finger-gate voltage and pulse amplitude at the N=0 to 1 transition, showing onset of transient current when the pulse exceeds the level splitting.

Average number of electrons per pulse cycle versus measurement pulse width τm, showing linear ground-state transport and saturation of excited-state transport; fit yields blocking and tunneling rates.
Three-level pulse sequence schematic with initialization, loading/holding, and readout steps for separating relaxation from measurement.
Average electrons per pulse cycle versus finger-gate voltage and holding time τh under the three-level pulse protocol, showing the relevant transport peaks.
Normalized excited-state survival probability versus holding time τh at B⊥ = 1.7 T, showing no resolvable decay within noise and only a lower bound on T1.
Normalized excited-state survival probability versus holding time τh at B⊥ = 2.4 T, showing weak decay corresponding to T1 around 50 μs.
Normalized excited-state survival probability versus holding time τh at B⊥ = 2.9 T, showing clear exponential decay with T1 around 5 μs.
Spin relaxation time T1 plotted versus spin splitting ΔE and magnetic field on a double-log scale, including a B^-8 guide to the eye and a shaded region where only lower bounds are possible.

paper_analysis.raw_measurement_details: The raw measurements are electrical transport traces collected on a BLG quantum dot device in a dilution refrigerator, including current as a function of gate voltage, pulse amplitude, pulse timing, and magnetic field. The paper specifically describes DC transport, finite-bias spectroscopy, and pulsed-gate transient current measurements; the raw instrument output is current versus gate/pulse parameters, not microscopy raw files.

dataset_characterization.data_availability_statement: The paper states: 'The data supporting the ﬁndings are available in a Zenodo repository under accession code https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.6599004.'
dataset_characterization.raw_data_description: The paper does not specify file formats or instrument file types. It implies raw electrical measurement data from transport experiments (current traces/maps versus VFG, VA, τm, τh, B⊥) and SEM imaging for the device figure, but does not describe the repository contents in detail.
dataset_characterization.processed_data_description: Processed data clearly includes extracted quantities such as ΔE versus B⊥, blocking rate γ, tunnel rate ΓD, normalized survival probabilities P↓(τh)/P↓(0), and fitted T1 values. The paper also refers to fit curves and lower bounds derived from comparison with calculated traces.
dataset_characterization.figure_data_description: Figure-specific data are presented in the main text as transport maps and curves; the paper does not explicitly say whether separate source-data files are provided for each figure, but the Zenodo repository is stated to contain supporting data. Supplementary figures are referenced (Supplementary Figs. 1, 2, 3, 4), suggesting additional figure-level datasets exist.
dataset_characterization.scripts_description: No analysis scripts, notebooks, or code are mentioned in the paper text.
dataset_characterization.notes: The dataset is primarily experimental transport data with derived fit parameters. The paper distinguishes raw measured current maps from processed quantities used to extract T1. Theoretical/schematic panels (device band diagrams and pulse sequences) are not data measurements. The text does not specify whether the Zenodo repository contains raw time traces, source-data tables, or only processed figure data; therefore the exact repository structure is unknown.

classification_prior.raw_data_evidence: The paper reports multiple experimental transport measurements: 'current through the QD as a function of the pulse amplitude VA and VFG', 'average number of electrons per pulse cycle', and 'data sets for different B⊥' used to extract T1.
classification_prior.source_data_evidence: The paper states that 'The data supporting the findings are available in a Zenodo repository' and presents figure-specific extracted curves and fits, indicating figure-ready supporting data are likely included.
classification_prior.data_availability_section_relevant: “The data supporting the ﬁndings are available in a Zenodo repository under accession code https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.6599004.”; “Supplementary information The online version contains supplementary material available at ...”; “The error bars correspond to the current noise level in the measurement.”
